options.B: FIDO2 authentication is cryptographically bound to the specific site origin, so a phishing site receives an assertion it cannot use
options.C: FIDO2 codes expire in 5 seconds, too fast for an attacker to relay
options.D: FIDO2 requires biometric verification that phishing proxies cannot capture

questions.explanation: FIDO2 authenticators sign a challenge that includes the current site's origin (RP ID). A phishing site at a different origin will receive a signed assertion that is only valid for that origin — the legitimate site will reject it. TOTP codes and push approvals are not bound to any origin, so an AiTM proxy can relay them in real time.

questions.question: An attacker uses msbuild.exe to compile and execute a C# payload inline, bypassing application whitelisting. What category of technique is this?

options.A: DLL injection
options.B: LOLBin (Living-off-the-Land Binary) abuse
options.C: Process hollowing
options.D: Reflective DLL loading

questions.explanation: LOLBins are legitimate, Microsoft-signed binaries that can execute arbitrary code and are therefore trusted by whitelisting solutions. msbuild.exe, certutil.exe, regsvr32.exe, and others are canonical examples. The defence is not blocking the binary (which breaks legitimate use) but detecting anomalous execution context via Sysmon.

questions.question: An Adversary-in-the-Middle (AiTM) phishing attack successfully captures a session cookie despite the user completing TOTP-based MFA. Which control would have prevented this specific attack?

options.A: Requiring a 30-day password rotation policy
options.B: Enforcing HSTS on the legitimate application
options.C: Deploying FIDO2 hardware security keys as the MFA factor
options.D: Using SMS-based OTP instead of TOTP authenticator apps

questions.explanation: AiTM captures the authenticated session cookie after the user completes MFA — the session cookie is replayable. FIDO2 prevents the authentication from completing at the phishing site because the origin is wrong. HSTS prevents HTTP downgrade but not HTTPS-to-HTTPS proxying. SMS OTP is weaker than TOTP, not stronger.

questions.question: Without LAPS, 300 domain-joined workstations all share the same local administrator password hash. An attacker compromises one workstation and extracts this hash. What is the immediate consequence?

options.A: The attacker can authenticate only to the compromised workstation until the password rotates
options.B: The attacker can authenticate to all 300 workstations using Pass-the-Hash without cracking the password
options.C: The attacker can authenticate to the Domain Controller using the same hash
options.D: The attacker needs to crack the hash before lateral movement is possible

questions.explanation: NTLM uses the hash directly for authentication — no password needed. A single local admin hash shared across all machines means one captured hash authenticates to all 300 machines. LAPS prevents this by making every machine's local admin password unique. Domain Controllers are not joined with local admin accounts in standard configurations.

questions.question: A Conditional Access policy triggers a step-up MFA challenge when a user signs in from an IP address in an unexpected country. What security principle does this policy implement?

options.A: Least privilege — restrict access based on the minimum required identity proof
options.B: Detection-based prevention — use a risk signal to require additional verification before granting access
options.C: Defence in depth — add a second perimeter firewall in front of the application
options.D: Zero trust — deny all access until complete network topology verification

questions.explanation: Conditional Access uses a detection signal (anomalous location) to trigger a preventive response (step-up MFA). This is the 'every preventive control paired with detection' principle applied at the authentication layer. It is not least privilege (which is about what you can do, not how you prove identity) nor a second perimeter firewall.

questions.question: A service account runs as 'Domain Admin' because 'it just worked when we set it up years ago'. Applying least privilege would change it to what, assuming it only needs to read files on one server?

options.A: A gMSA with Domain Admin membership but auto-rotating password
options.B: A standard domain user account with read NTFS permission on the specific folder only
options.C: A Virtual Account (NT SERVICE\ServiceName) to eliminate domain exposure entirely
options.D: A local administrator account to reduce domain-wide exposure

questions.explanation: Least privilege: minimum rights for the task, at the narrowest scope. File read on one server requires a domain user account with only read NTFS ACL on that folder — no Domain Admin. A Virtual Account cannot access network resources. A gMSA is overkill for single-server local file read. Local admin grants excessive local privilege.

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'every preventive control must be paired with detection'. Use one specific bypass technique from this module and show how detection would catch the bypass.

required_points: Preventive controls can be bypassed — no control is 100% effective
Without detection, a bypass goes unnoticed for an extended period (dwell time)
Specific bypass example: AV bypass via custom shellcode / LOLBin / AiTM MFA bypass
Detection mechanism that catches the bypass: Sysmon process injection events / Sysmon Event 1 for LOLBin anomalous execution / impossible travel Conditional Access signal
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Any bypass technique from L29-L30 paired with a plausible detection mechanism. The key requirement is that the pair is specific — not generic 'use SIEM'.
answer_key.full_credit: Names a specific bypass, explains why the preventive control fails, names the detection mechanism that catches it, and explains how it catches it.
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct principle stated but bypass/detection pair is vague or only one half is addressed.

questions.question: What are the five highest-signal SIEM alert rules for a new security monitoring programme? For each, state the detection mechanism (event ID and field) and the attack it catches.

required_points: 1. Office app → cmd.exe/powershell.exe (Sysmon Event 1 parent-child chain) → macro malware
2. NTLM V2 network logon from workstation to DC (Security 4624, LogonType=3, LmPackageName=NTLM V2) → Pass-the-Hash
3. lsass.exe OpenProcess from non-SYSTEM process (Sysmon Event 10, TargetImage=lsass.exe) → credential dumping
4. New service with user-writable ImagePath (Security 7045, ImagePath contains Temp/AppData/Public) → persistence
5. DNS query volume anomaly or long random subdomains (Sysmon Event 22) → C2/exfiltration
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Order does not matter. AiTM detection via Conditional Access impossible travel is acceptable for rule 5. Event IDs from L25-L27 are also acceptable alternatives.
answer_key.full_credit: All five rules named with specific detection mechanism and attack technique for each.
answer_key.partial_credit: Three or four rules with mechanisms, or five rules with vague mechanisms.

questions.prompt: A CISO presents their security posture: 'We have Defender Antivirus, TOTP-based MFA on all accounts, and a next-generation firewall at the perimeter.' Critically evaluate this posture using evidence from this module. What specific attack techniques bypass each control, and what three hardening additions would you recommend first?
questions.guidance: A strong response is evidence-based — specific bypass techniques, not just 'sophisticated attackers can bypass AV'. Recommendations should be prioritised by impact and linked to the module's key claims. Generic advice ('patch more', 'train users') should be penalised without specifics.

rubric.A: Names specific bypass for AV (LOLBin, custom shellcode via Sysmon Event 1), TOTP MFA (AiTM proxy), firewall (DNS C2, LOLBin outbound via trusted processes); three recommendations are specific (e.g. FIDO2 keys, Sysmon deployment, LAPS) with impact reasoning tied to module claims
rubric.B: Identifies most bypasses; recommendations are correct but partially generic; makes the detection pairing argument
rubric.C: Mentions bypasses exist but is vague; recommendations are generic (patch, train users); no evidence-based reasoning

M01.title: Module Check: Network Protocol Security Fundamentals

questions.question: Describe the mental model for evaluating a network protocol's attack surface based on its design features.
questions.answer: Ask: (1) Does the protocol authenticate peers, or trust by address? (2) Is data encrypted, or plaintext? (3) Is there a stateless trust mechanism an attacker can spoof? Each unanswered "yes" is a potential attack vector. ARP trusts by design (no auth); TCP trusts by sequence number (forgeable); TLS adds auth + encryption.

questions.question: Explain the ARP spoofing mechanism. What condition enables it and what is its consequence?
questions.answer: ARP has no authentication. An attacker broadcasts a gratuitous ARP reply mapping their MAC to the victim's IP. LAN hosts cache this mapping and forward traffic to the attacker — enabling man-in-the-middle interception. Condition: attacker must be on the same broadcast domain.

questions.question: How does TCP RST injection work and what does it allow an attacker to do?
questions.answer: An attacker forges a TCP RST packet with a valid sequence number from within the current window, addressed to one endpoint. The receiving host treats it as a legitimate connection termination and drops the session. This enables denial-of-service against long-lived connections (BGP, SSH) if the attacker can observe sequence numbers.

questions.question: Which command verifies that an ARP entry was populated by a legitimate host (not a spoofed reply)?

options.A: ping -a <ip>
options.B: arp -a && nmap --script broadcast-arp
options.C: traceroute <ip>
options.D: netstat -rn

questions.explanation: `arp -a` shows the local ARP cache. `nmap --script broadcast-arp` sends ARP probes and reports all hosts that respond, allowing comparison. If two different MACs claim the same IP, spoofing is occurring. ping, traceroute, and netstat do not expose ARP cache entries directly.

questions.question: What is the primary security limitation of trusting IP addresses for access control?

options.A: IP addresses change too frequently to be reliable
options.B: IP source addresses can be spoofed in packets, especially for protocols without handshake state
options.C: IPv4 addresses are publicly enumerable
options.D: NAT prevents accurate IP attribution

questions.explanation: IP source addresses are set by the sender and not verified by intermediate routers (BCP38 filtering helps but is not universal). Stateless protocols (UDP) are trivially spoofed. TCP's three-way handshake raises the bar but blind injection is still possible from the same network segment.

M02.title: Module Check: DNS Security and Intelligence

questions.question: What two DNS transaction properties made classic DNS susceptible to cache poisoning, and what did the Kaminsky attack exploit?
questions.answer: Predictable 16-bit transaction ID (65,536 possibilities) and source port reuse (effectively reducing entropy further). Kaminsky flooded a resolver with forged responses for a target domain before the legitimate answer arrived, racing to match both the transaction ID and source port. DNSSEC validation prevents this; source port randomisation raises the attack cost significantly.

questions.question: What is a Domain Generation Algorithm (DGA) and what NXDOMAIN pattern reveals its activity?
questions.answer: A DGA programmatically generates many domain names (e.g., using a time-based seed). Malware queries these sequentially until one is registered by the C2 operator. The pattern: high volume of NXDOMAIN responses from a single host for algorithmically similar (pseudo-random) domains, often at regular intervals.

questions.question: How does a CAA (Certification Authority Authorisation) record improve certificate issuance security?
questions.answer: A CAA record specifies which CAs are permitted to issue certificates for a domain. Compliant CAs check for a CAA record before issuing. If an attacker tricks a rogue CA into issuing a fraudulent certificate, the certificate is policy-violating (detectable via Certificate Transparency logs). CAA does not prevent issuance by non-compliant CAs.

questions.question: Which Sysmon event ID logs DNS query and response activity?

options.A: Event 1 (Process Create)
options.B: Event 3 (Network Connection)
options.C: Event 22 (DNS Query)
options.D: Event 10 (Process Access)

questions.explanation: Sysmon Event 22 captures DNS query events: the queried name, response type, and result. It is essential for detecting DGA activity and C2 beaconing. Event 3 captures TCP/UDP connections (IP + port, not DNS names). Events 1 and 10 are process-related.

questions.question: What is the correct DNS server configuration to prevent zone transfer to arbitrary hosts?

options.A: Set TTL to 0 on all zone records
options.B: Restrict AXFR transfers to specific authorised secondary server IPs using ACLs
options.C: Disable DNSSEC to avoid exposing zone walk data
options.D: Enable NSEC3 with opt-out on the zone

questions.explanation: Zone transfers (AXFR) export the entire DNS zone — a goldmine for reconnaissance. The fix is an ACL that allows AXFR only from authorised secondary nameservers. DNSSEC does not prevent zone transfers and should not be disabled. NSEC3 mitigates zone enumeration via DNSSEC but is unrelated to AXFR.

M03.title: Module Check: TLS and Cryptographic Protocol Security

questions.question: Name three attacks against CBC mode in TLS and state the underlying weakness each exploits.
questions.answer: BEAST: predictable IV (chained from previous ciphertext) enables chosen-plaintext attack against TLS 1.0. Lucky 13: timing side-channel in CBC-HMAC padding validation leaks plaintext. POODLE: SSL 3.0 CBC padding is ambiguous — attacker forces downgrade then exploits padding oracle. Root cause: CBC requires padding validation, which creates timing and oracle vulnerabilities.

questions.question: What is AEAD encryption and why does TLS 1.3 mandate it?
questions.answer: AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data) provides confidentiality and integrity in one operation — the ciphertext includes an authentication tag. TLS 1.3 removed all non-AEAD cipher suites because they require separate MAC computation, which historically created side-channels (Lucky 13, BEAST). AEAD eliminates the padding oracle class entirely.

questions.question: What happens if the same AES-GCM nonce is reused with the same key, and why is this catastrophic?
questions.answer: AES-GCM computes a keystream as AES(key, nonce || counter). Reusing nonce+key produces the same keystream. XORing two ciphertexts encrypted with the same keystream cancels it out, revealing C1⊕C2 = P1⊕P2. With cribs, both plaintexts can be recovered. The auth tag also becomes forgeable. Nonce reuse completely breaks GCM.

questions.question: Which cipher suites does TLS 1.3 support (all others are removed)?

options.A: AES-CBC and RC4
options.B: AES-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305
options.C: AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305, and AES-CCM
options.D: AES-GCM only

questions.explanation: TLS 1.3 mandates AEAD-only suites. The three mandated are: AES-128-GCM-SHA256, AES-256-GCM-SHA384, and ChaCha20-Poly1305-SHA256. AES-CCM is also in the spec for constrained devices (RFC 8446). CBC and RC4 are removed entirely.

questions.question: What does "forward secrecy" guarantee that static RSA key exchange does not?

options.A: The server certificate cannot be forged
options.B: Session keys cannot be decrypted even if the server's long-term private key is later compromised
options.C: The client is authenticated to the server
options.D: Traffic is encrypted with 256-bit keys

questions.explanation: Forward secrecy (via ephemeral Diffie-Hellman) generates a fresh session key per session. The private key is discarded after the handshake. An attacker who later steals the server's long-term key cannot decrypt previously recorded sessions. Static RSA sends the session key encrypted with the server's long-term public key — steal the key, decrypt everything.

questions.question: Explain forward secrecy. What ephemeral key exchange achieves it and what does it protect?
questions.answer: Forward secrecy uses Ephemeral Diffie-Hellman (ECDHE or DHE) to generate a fresh session key per connection. The private key is discarded after the handshake. If the server's long-term private key is later stolen, previously recorded sessions remain undecryptable — the session key never existed outside RAM.

questions.question: What bcrypt cost factor does OWASP recommend in 2024, and what is the reasoning?
questions.answer: OWASP recommends cost factor 12 (bcrypt) or higher. The reasoning: bcrypt cost factor is a work parameter — each increment doubles the computation. Factor 12 produces ~200–400ms hashing time on a modern CPU, making offline brute force slow (millions of hashes/second is infeasible at this cost). Lower factors (8, 10) were calibrated for older hardware.

questions.question: What are the three parameters of Argon2id and what does each control?
questions.answer: Memory cost (m): kilobytes of RAM required per hash — resists GPU/ASIC parallelism. Time cost (t): number of iterations — resists fast CPUs. Parallelism (p): number of threads — tuned to server capability. Argon2id combines Argon2i (side-channel resistance) and Argon2d (GPU resistance).

questions.question: How many certificate chain checks does RFC 5280 require for path validation?
questions.explanation: RFC 5280 path validation performs 4 checks per certificate in the chain: (1) signature valid against issuer, (2) current time within validity period, (3) certificate not revoked (CRL/OCSP), (4) certificate usage matches intended purpose (key usage, extended key usage). All 4 must pass.

questions.question: What attack does salting a password hash prevent?

options.A: Timing attacks on hash comparison
options.B: Rainbow table lookups and precomputed hash database attacks
options.C: Brute-force dictionary attacks
options.D: Length-extension attacks

questions.explanation: A random salt, unique per user, ensures that two users with the same password have different hash values. This defeats precomputed rainbow tables and shared hash databases — an attacker must brute-force each hash individually. Salts do not prevent brute force (that's the work factor's job), timing attacks (constant-time comparison's job), or length extension (HMAC's job).

M05.title: Module Check: Linux Persistence and Privilege Escalation

questions.question: How does the SUID bit allow a low-privilege user to execute code as root?
questions.answer: The SUID bit on an executable sets the effective UID to the file owner (e.g., root) when executed, regardless of who runs it. The process inherits root privileges for its duration. If the binary allows code execution (e.g., a shell, vim, find with -exec), the attacker gains a root shell.

questions.question: Write the `find` command that lists all SUID-bit files on a Linux system.
questions.answer: `find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null` — searches from root, matches files with the SUID permission bit set (4000 in octal), suppresses permission errors. Adding `-ls` gives ownership details.

questions.question: Name four locations an attacker uses to establish persistence on Linux.
questions.answer: (1) ~/.bashrc or ~/.profile — runs on user login. (2) /etc/cron.d/ or crontab — scheduled execution. (3) systemd unit file in /etc/systemd/system/ — survives reboot. (4) LD_PRELOAD in /etc/ld.so.preload — injects a shared library into every process. Also: SSH authorized_keys, /etc/rc.local.

questions.question: What detection benefit does the Linux Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS) provide for threat hunting?

options.A: FHS enforces file permission standards that block malware writes
options.B: FHS defines expected locations — executables outside standard paths are anomalous and easier to detect
options.C: FHS checksums system binaries at boot
options.D: FHS prevents modification of /etc without root

questions.explanation: FHS defines conventions: binaries in /usr/bin, libraries in /usr/lib, config in /etc. An executable in /tmp, /var/www, or a home directory is an immediate anomaly. Threat hunters use this baseline to filter noise from true positives. FHS has no enforcement mechanism — it is a convention, not a control.

questions.question: What is the difference between privilege escalation and lateral movement?

options.A: Privilege escalation moves between hosts; lateral movement elevates within one host
options.B: Privilege escalation elevates access level on the current host; lateral movement moves to a different host
options.C: Both terms describe the same technique at different stages of the kill chain
options.D: Lateral movement is only possible after full privilege escalation

questions.explanation: Privilege escalation: low-priv user → root on the same machine. Lateral movement: compromised host → another host in the network. Both are post-exploitation techniques but target different attack dimensions. Lateral movement does not require root; credential theft often enables lateral movement at user level.

questions.question: Name the three components of a WMI event subscription used for persistence and the Sysmon events that reveal them.
questions.answer: __EventFilter: defines the trigger condition (e.g., every 60 seconds). __EventConsumer: the action to execute (CommandLineEventConsumer runs a command). __FilterToConsumerBinding: links filter to consumer. Sysmon Event 19 (WmiEventFilter), Event 20 (WmiEventConsumer), Event 21 (WmiEventConsumerToFilter) capture the creation of each.

questions.question: Name two Windows Registry paths commonly abused for persistence.
questions.answer: HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run — executes on user login without admin. HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run — executes for all users on login (requires admin). Also: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services (service creation), HKCU\...\RunOnce (one-time execution).

questions.question: What is the exclusion-list pattern in malware persistence and why is it stealthy?
questions.answer: Malware adds its process, file path, or hash to Windows Defender's exclusion list before executing — preventing AV scanning of the payload. It is stealthy because the exclusion entry looks like a legitimate administrative action, and many EDRs do not alert on exclusion additions by default.

questions.question: Why are WMI subscriptions a preferred persistence mechanism over Registry Run keys for sophisticated actors?

options.A: WMI subscriptions survive registry cleanups and do not appear in autoruns
options.B: WMI subscriptions execute faster than Registry Run keys
options.C: WMI subscriptions are stored in a separate database and survive Autoruns-based detection
options.D: WMI subscriptions do not require admin privileges

questions.explanation: WMI subscriptions are stored in the WMI repository (%SystemRoot%\System32\wbem\Repository), not in the registry or filesystem. Tools like Autoruns and most registry viewers miss them. This makes WMI subscriptions harder to enumerate and clean. They also survive reboots and registry sanitisation.

questions.question: Which Windows mechanism does the exclusion-list persistence technique abuse?

options.A: Windows Firewall inbound rule exceptions
options.B: Windows Defender antivirus scan exclusions
options.C: AppLocker allowed publisher rules
options.D: UAC virtualisation registry redirection

questions.explanation: Adding a path or process to Windows Defender's exclusion list prevents it from being scanned. Malware writes its own path to `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Exclusions\Paths` before execution. Legitimate software (AV, backup agents) uses this API — making malicious additions blend in with normal admin activity.

M07.title: Module Check: Windows Event Log Analysis

questions.question: What do the LogonType values in Event 4624 indicate? List at least three.
questions.answer: LogonType 2: Interactive (local console). LogonType 3: Network (e.g., SMB, WMI). LogonType 10: RemoteInteractive (RDP). LogonType 5: Service logon. LogonType 7: Unlock. Type 3 is most common for lateral movement; Type 10 for RDP-based attacks.

questions.question: How does Sysmon Event 10 (ProcessAccess) detect LSASS memory dumping?
questions.answer: Event 10 fires when one process opens a handle to another process's memory. Credential dumpers (Mimikatz, procdump) open LSASS with PROCESS_VM_READ access. Filter for: TargetImage = lsass.exe, GrantedAccess = 0x1010 or 0x1410 (VM read permissions). This detects Mimikatz and most LSASS dump tools.

questions.question: What is the DCSync attack and which Windows events detect it?
questions.answer: DCSync abuses the MS-DRSR (Directory Replication Service Remote) protocol to request password hashes from a DC as if the attacker were another DC — without needing to run code on the DC. Detection: Event 4662 on the DC (DsGetNCChanges — object access with GUID 1131f6aa...) and Sysmon 3 (outbound replication port 135/3268 from non-DC).

questions.question: Why is ProcessGUID a better correlation field than PID in Sysmon logs?

options.A: PIDs are randomised by the OS to prevent tracking
options.B: PIDs are reused after process termination; ProcessGUID is globally unique for the process lifetime
options.C: ProcessGUID includes the parent process, making tree reconstruction easier
options.D: PIDs are not logged in Sysmon

questions.explanation: Windows reuses PIDs shortly after a process exits. If a malicious process exits and a benign process gets the same PID, correlating events by PID would falsely link them. ProcessGUID is a UUID assigned at process creation and never reused — allowing reliable cross-event correlation throughout the process lifetime.

questions.question: In the PsExec attack kill chain, which four event sources provide detection coverage?

options.A: Event 4624, Event 7045, Sysmon 1, Sysmon 3
options.B: Event 4662, Event 4768, Sysmon 10, Sysmon 22
options.C: Event 4625, Event 4740, Sysmon 7, Sysmon 19
options.D: Event 4648, Event 4771, Sysmon 11, Sysmon 17

questions.explanation: PsExec kill chain: Sysmon 3 (lateral SMB connection), Event 4624 LogonType 3 (network authentication), Event 7045 (PSEXESVC service installation), Sysmon 1 (cmd.exe/payload process spawned by PSEXESVC). These four events across two log sources cover the full chain.

M08.title: Module Check: Credential Attacks and Kerberos

questions.question: Why is the NTLM hash functionally equivalent to a plaintext password for authentication?
questions.answer: NTLM authentication uses challenge-response: the client proves identity by hashing a server challenge with the NT hash. An attacker with the NT hash can compute the correct response without knowing the original password. They never need to crack it — they authenticate directly. This is the pass-the-hash (PtH) attack.

questions.question: What Event 4624 signature distinguishes a pass-the-hash authentication from a normal network login?
questions.answer: PtH produces a Type 3 (Network) logon with AuthenticationPackageName = NTLM and LogonProcessName = NtLmSsp. The connecting IP is the attacker's machine. A normal Kerberos login uses AuthenticationPackageName = Kerberos. Alert on NTLM Type 3 logons from non-standard hosts, especially to high-value targets.

questions.question: Why does Kerberoasting target service accounts specifically, and what makes RC4 the preferred encryption for the attack?
questions.answer: Service accounts have SPNs (Service Principal Names) — any domain user can request a Kerberos service ticket encrypted with the service account's password hash. The attacker takes the ticket offline and cracks it. RC4-HMAC (type 23) is preferred because it is faster to crack than AES-256 by several orders of magnitude. Forcing RC4 downgrade is part of the attack.

questions.question: How does setting PowerShell execution policy to "Restricted" defend against script-based attacks?

options.A: It prevents all PowerShell execution, including LOL (living-off-the-land) attacks
options.B: It is a meaningful security boundary that blocks unsigned scripts
options.C: It is bypassable with `-ExecutionPolicy Bypass` or `powershell -enc` and provides no real security
options.D: It prevents script injection only from remote sources

questions.explanation: Execution policy is not a security boundary — it is a convenience feature. Any local admin or the current user can bypass it with `powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass script.ps1` or by encoding the script. AMSI (Antimalware Scan Interface) and AppLocker are the actual controls for restricting PowerShell script execution.

questions.question: What is the "paired-detection principle" for credential attacks?

options.A: Always deploy two EDR agents to ensure coverage
options.B: Pair each attack technique with two independent detection methods from different data sources
options.C: Correlate authentication events from both the source and destination host
options.D: Alert only when two separate threat intelligence feeds both flag a host

questions.explanation: The paired-detection principle: for each technique, implement at least two detections from independent data sources (e.g., Event 4624 and Sysmon 3 for lateral movement). If one detection is evaded or the log source is disabled, the second provides coverage. Single-source detection has a single point of failure.

questions.q: What Windows Event ID detects a new service being installed, and where does it appear?
questions.a: Event 7045 in the System log. It is generated by the Service Control Manager when a new service is registered. Alert when the ImagePath points to user-writable locations like %Temp%, %AppData%, or C:\Users\Public.

questions.q: What three pieces of information does Sysmon Event 1 capture that Windows Event 4688 does not provide in a default configuration?
questions.a: Full command line, parent process image (and parent command line), and SHA256 hash of the executable — all in a single record. Event 4688 requires separate audit policy settings to get command line, provides no hashes, and requires event correlation for parent process information.

questions.q: Explain COM hijacking persistence and why HKCU registration requires no admin rights.
questions.a: COM hijacking plants a malicious DLL as the handler for a COM class used by a legitimate elevated process. HKCU\SOFTWARE\Classes\CLSID is writable by the current user (no admin required) and is searched before HKLM\SOFTWARE\Classes\CLSID. When an elevated process loads that COM object, it finds the HKCU registration first and loads the attacker's DLL in the elevated process context.

questions.q: Why does detecting Windows persistence require baselining rather than just monitoring known persistence locations?
questions.a: Every autostart location already contains many legitimate entries. Malware adds one more entry with a plausible-looking name. Without a baseline, every entry requires manual investigation. With a baseline of known-good state, detection is a delta check — any new entry not in the baseline triggers an alert. Autoruns with -h (hide Microsoft-signed) reduces noise by filtering trusted entries.

questions.q: What parent-child process chain in Sysmon Event 1 indicates document macro malware, and what confirms lateral movement is imminent?
questions.a: WINWORD.EXE → cmd.exe → powershell.exe (with -enc [base64] argument). Confirmation of imminent lateral movement: Sysmon Event 3 (NetworkConnect) showing the PowerShell process making outbound connections immediately after creation — indicating it has downloaded and is executing a staged payload.

M10.title: Defence in Depth and Modern Hardening — Quick Check

questions.q: What is the principle of least privilege? State it precisely including scope, duration, and method.
questions.a: Every principal (user, process, service) should hold the minimum privilege required to perform its authorised function, for only the duration that function requires, at the narrowest scope where that privilege satisfies the requirement. In practice: read-only where possible, named accounts over shared accounts, time-limited privilege elevation over permanent admin membership.

questions.q: What specific protections does the Protected Users security group enforce for Domain Admin accounts?
questions.a: Five protections: (1) No NTLM authentication — Kerberos only, defeating Pass-the-Hash. (2) No credential caching — cannot log in offline. (3) AES-only Kerberos encryption — no RC4, preventing crackable Kerberoasting ticket hashes. (4) TGT lifetime limited to 4 hours. (5) No Kerberos unconstrained delegation.

questions.q: Why is FIDO2 the only phishing-resistant MFA factor, while TOTP and push notifications are not?
questions.a: FIDO2 uses origin-bound asymmetric cryptography. The authenticator signs a challenge that includes the current site's origin (RP ID). A phishing site at a different origin cannot obtain a valid assertion — the authenticator rejects it. TOTP codes and push notifications are not bound to any origin, so an AiTM proxy can forward them to the legitimate site in real time.

questions.q: What is MTTD and why does closing the MTTD gap matter more than eliminating specific attack techniques?
questions.a: MTTD = Mean Time to Detection. Without mature detection, dwell time averages weeks to months — during which attackers establish persistence, harvest credentials, and move laterally. Reducing MTTD to hours limits what attackers can accomplish before discovery. No prevention control eliminates all attack techniques; detection is the control that bounds damage regardless of which technique succeeds.

questions.q: A service account currently runs as Domain Admin. Using the least privilege principle, what should it run as, and what specific steps would you take?
questions.a: Run as a standard domain user account or gMSA with only the specific permissions the service needs. Steps: (1) Identify what the service actually accesses (files, databases, network shares). (2) Create a dedicated service account. (3) Grant only the required NTFS/database/network permissions. (4) If cross-machine domain access is needed, use a gMSA for auto-rotating passwords. (5) Remove from Domain Admin and any other high-privilege groups. (6) Test that the service still functions.

M02.title: Coding Challenge: DNS Attacks and Defense

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `detect_dga_pattern(queries)` that analyzes a list of DNS query domain names and identifies whether they exhibit DGA (Domain Generation Algorithm) characteristics.

## DGA Characteristics

DGA domains typically have:
- High consonant-to-vowel ratio (more random)
- Unusual lengths (not typical business domains)
- Non-word patterns (no recognizable English words)

## Function Behavior

Receives a list of full domain names like `['abc123.com', 'hello.com', 'qwxyz.org']`

Returns `True` if the pattern matches DGA characteristics, `False` otherwise.

## DGA Detection Criteria

A domain is considered 'likely DGA' if **any** of these conditions are true:
- At least 3 consecutive consonants appear
- Vowels make up less than 30% of the domain name (excluding TLD)
- The domain name (excluding TLD) is longer than 12 characters or shorter than 4 characters
challenges.starter_code:
def detect_dga_pattern(queries):
    """
    Analyze a list of DNS query domains for DGA characteristics.
    Returns True if the pattern suggests DGA activity, False otherwise.
    """
    pass

test_cases.name: Obvious DGA pattern - high consonant density

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `parse_dns_records(record_string)` that parses a DNS record string and extracts structured components.

## Input Format

Raw DNS record strings like:
- `example.com. 3600 IN A 192.0.2.1`
- `example.com. 3600 IN CAA 0 issue "letsencrypt.org"`
- `example.com. 3600 IN MX 10 mail.example.com`

## Output Format

Return a dictionary with keys: `'domain'`, `'ttl'`, `'type'`, `'value'`

## Field Descriptions

- **domain**: the FQDN with trailing dot removed
- **ttl**: time-to-live as an integer
- **type**: the record type (A, AAAA, CAA, MX, TXT, etc.)
- **value**: the record data (IP, flags/tag/value for CAA, etc.)
challenges.starter_code:
def parse_dns_records(record_string):
    """
    Parse a DNS record string into structured components.
    Returns a dictionary with domain, ttl, type, value.
    """
    pass

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `validate_bcrypt_params(bcrypt_hash)` that analyzes a bcrypt hash string and validates it meets OWASP security recommendations.

## Hash Format

Bcrypt hashes follow: `$2b$cost$salt+hash`

Example: `$2b$12$R9h/cIPz0gi.URNNBU3faPbVi/rrq.yJZaoxbE5qXkiTLcKj19PBa`

## Return Value

Return a dictionary with:
- `'valid'`: True/False (meets OWASP minimum)
- `'cost'`: extracted cost factor (as integer)
- `'reason'`: explanation if invalid (empty string if valid)

## Validation Rules

- OWASP minimum requirement: cost factor ≥ 12
- If cost < 12: return reason `'cost_too_low'`
- If hash format is malformed: return reason `'invalid_format'`
challenges.starter_code:
def validate_bcrypt_params(bcrypt_hash):
    """
    Validate a bcrypt hash meets OWASP security requirements.
    Returns a dictionary with 'valid', 'cost', and 'reason' keys.
    """
    pass

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `validate_cert_chain(certificates)` that validates a certificate chain following RFC 5280 requirements.

## Input Format

Each certificate is a dictionary with:
- `'subject'`: domain/CN of the certificate
- `'issuer'`: who signed it
- `'signature_valid'`: True/False (already verified)
- `'valid_from'`: year cert becomes valid
- `'valid_until'`: year cert expires
- `'revoked'`: True/False (revocation status)
- `'is_root'`: True if this is a root CA cert

## RFC 5280 Validation Checks

1. **Signature validation**: signature_valid must be True
2. **Validity period**: current year (2026) must be between valid_from and valid_until
3. **Revocation status**: revoked must be False
4. **Path**: at least one cert must be root (is_root: True)

## Return Value

Return a dictionary: `{'valid': True/False, 'failed_checks': [list of failed check names]}`
challenges.starter_code:
def validate_cert_chain(certificates):
    """
    Validate certificate chain against RFC 5280 requirements.
    Returns dictionary with 'valid' and 'failed_checks' keys.
    Current year is assumed to be 2026.
    """
    pass

test_cases.name: Valid chain
test_cases.input: [{'subject': 'example.com', 'issuer': 'LetsEncrypt', 'signature_valid': True, 'valid_from': 2024, 'valid_until': 2027, 'revoked': False, 'is_root': False}, {'subject': 'LetsEncrypt', 'issuer': 'DST Root', 'signature_valid': True, 'valid_from': 2020, 'valid_until': 2035, 'revoked': False, 'is_root': True}]

test_cases.name: Revoked certificate
test_cases.input: [{'subject': 'old.example.com', 'issuer': 'LetsEncrypt', 'signature_valid': True, 'valid_from': 2024, 'valid_until': 2027, 'revoked': True, 'is_root': False}, {'subject': 'LetsEncrypt', 'issuer': 'DST Root', 'signature_valid': True, 'valid_from': 2020, 'valid_until': 2035, 'revoked': False, 'is_root': True}]

test_cases.name: Invalid signature
test_cases.input: [{'subject': 'example.com', 'issuer': 'LetsEncrypt', 'signature_valid': False, 'valid_from': 2024, 'valid_until': 2027, 'revoked': False, 'is_root': False}, {'subject': 'LetsEncrypt', 'issuer': 'DST Root', 'signature_valid': True, 'valid_from': 2020, 'valid_until': 2035, 'revoked': False, 'is_root': True}]

M05.title: Coding Challenge: Linux Permissions and Security

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `identify_suid_files(ls_output_lines)` that analyzes `ls -l` output and identifies files with the SUID bit set.

## SUID Bit Indicator

The SUID bit appears as 's' in the owner execute position:
- `-rwsr-xr-x` has SUID set (the 's' in positions 1-3)
- `-rwxr-xr-x` does NOT have SUID set

## Function Behavior

Receives a list of strings from `ls -l` output and returns a list of filenames that have SUID set, in the order they appear.

## Example

Input line: `-rwsr-xr-x 1 root root 1234 Jan 1 00:00 /usr/bin/passwd`

Extract the filename (rightmost field) if the permissions show SUID.
challenges.starter_code:
def identify_suid_files(ls_output_lines):
    """
    Identify files with SUID bit set from ls -l output.
    Returns a list of filenames with SUID set.
    """
    pass

test_cases.name: Multiple files, only one SUID
test_cases.input: ['-rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 123 Jan 1 00:00 /bin/ls', '-rwsr-xr-x 1 root root 456 Jan 1 00:00 /usr/bin/sudo', '-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 789 Jan 1 00:00 /etc/passwd']

test_cases.name: No SUID files
test_cases.input: ['-rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 123 Jan 1 00:00 /bin/ls', '-rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 456 Jan 1 00:00 /bin/cat']

test_cases.name: Multiple SUID files
test_cases.input: ['-rwsr-xr-x 1 root root 100 Jan 1 00:00 /usr/bin/passwd', '-rwsr-xr-x 1 root root 200 Jan 1 00:00 /bin/su', '-rwxr-xr-x 1 user user 300 Jan 1 00:00 /usr/bin/vim', '-rwsr-xr-x 1 root root 400 Jan 1 00:00 /usr/bin/chfn']

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `find_overprivileged_files(ls_output_lines)` that identifies potentially dangerous permissions on files.

## Overprivileged Conditions

A file is considered 'overprivileged' if **any** of these are true:

1. **world_writable**: Other bit includes 'w' (e.g., `-rw-rw-rw-`)
2. **group_writable_exec**: Group bits are 'w' and execute is 'x' (e.g., `-rwxrwx---`)
3. **suid_in_tmp**: SUID on a file in /tmp or /var/tmp
4. **suid_unprivileged**: SUID executable owned by unprivileged user (UID != root)

## Return Value

Receives `ls -l` output lines and returns a list of tuples:

`(filename, reason)`

Where reason is one of the four conditions above.
challenges.starter_code:
def find_overprivileged_files(ls_output_lines):
    """
    Identify files with potentially dangerous permissions.
    Returns a list of (filename, reason) tuples.
    """
    pass

test_cases.name: Normal files (safe)
test_cases.input: ['-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 100 Jan 1 00:00 /etc/passwd', '-rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 200 Jan 1 00:00 /bin/ls']

test_cases.name: Mixed permissions
test_cases.input: ['-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 100 Jan 1 00:00 /etc/passwd', '-rw-rw-rw- 1 user user 200 Jan 1 00:00 /var/log/app.log', '-rwsr-xr-x 1 myuser myuser 300 Jan 1 00:00 /usr/local/bin/tool']

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `parse_crontab(crontab_entries)` that analyzes crontab lines and identifies suspicious entries.

## Crontab Format

Each line follows: `minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week user command`

Example: `0 3 * * * root /tmp/cleanup.sh`

## Function Behavior

Receives a list of crontab lines and returns a list of suspicious entries as tuples:

`(line_number, reason, command)`

## Suspicious Patterns

1. **suspicious_path**: command starts with /tmp, /var/tmp, /dev/shm, or ~
2. **hidden_command**: command starts with . or contains ..
3. **early_hour**: runs between 2 AM and 4 AM (common for malware)
4. **obfuscated_cmd**: command contains pipes to base64, xxd, or hex decoders
challenges.starter_code:
def parse_crontab(crontab_entries):
    """
    Analyze crontab entries for suspicious patterns.
    Returns a list of (line_number, reason, command) tuples for suspicious entries.
    """
    pass

test_cases.name: Obfuscated command
test_cases.expected_output: [(1, 'early_hour', 'echo payload | base64 -d | bash'), (1, 'obfuscated_cmd', 'echo payload | base64 -d | bash')]

challenges.prompt:
Write a function `correlate_events(event_logs)` that analyzes a sequence of Windows event log entries and identifies suspicious authentication patterns.

## Input Format

Each event is a dictionary with:
- `'event_id'`: unique identifier
- `'logon_type'`: authentication type code
- `'protocol'`: Kerberos, NTLM, etc.
- `'ip'`: source IP address
- `'status'`: 'success' or 'failed' (optional)

## Suspicious Patterns to Detect

1. **network_logon_suspicious**: LogonType 3 (network) followed by process access to lsass.exe
2. **rdp_then_execution**: LogonType 10 (RDP) followed by unusual process execution
3. **anomalous_protocol**: any logon with 'NTLM' protocol (should be Kerberos in domain environments)
4. **failed_followed_by_success**: same IP/user with failed auth then success within 5 events

## Return Value

Return a list of tuples:

`(event_id, issue_type)`

Events are provided in order of occurrence.
challenges.starter_code:
def correlate_events(event_logs):
    """
    Correlate Windows events to identify suspicious authentication patterns.
    Returns a list of (event_id, issue_type) tuples for suspicious patterns.
    """
    pass

test_cases.name: Failed then successful auth from same IP

M01.title: Practical applications — How Security Thinking Works

applications.title: Protocol Threat Model
applications.summary: Build a one-page threat model for a protocol of your choice using the trust-assumption method from L02.
applications.prompt: Select any network protocol (e.g. SMTP, FTP, OAuth 2.0). For each protocol feature, identify: (1) what the protocol trusts, (2) who supplies that trusted value, and (3) what an attacker can do if they control that value. Produce a table with columns: Feature | Trust Assumption | Attacker-Controlled Input | Attack Consequence.
applications.deliverable: A completed threat model table with a minimum of five protocol features analysed.

M02.title: Practical applications — Networking Protocols and Their Attacks

applications.title: Network Attack Surface Audit
applications.summary: Map the attack surface of a home or lab network using the L04–L06 attack taxonomy.
applications.prompt: Using a home router, lab VM, or packet capture file: (1) identify whether ARP is authenticated on the LAN segment, (2) enumerate HTTP/HTTPS services and check for Host header reflection, (3) document whether a SYN backlog limit exists. For each finding state the attack vector it enables.
applications.deliverable: A three-section audit report (ARP, HTTP, TCP) with a finding and risk rating for each.

M03.title: Practical applications — DNS — The Universal Attack, Defense, and Monitoring Layer

applications.title: DNS Security Hardening Checklist
applications.summary: Produce a DNS hardening checklist for a hypothetical organisation and validate it against a real domain.
applications.prompt: For a domain you control or a public domain you have permission to analyse: (1) check whether DNSSEC is enabled, (2) verify CAA records are present, (3) test whether zone transfers are restricted, (4) check if Sysmon Event 22 (or equivalent) is available for DNS query logging. Document the current state and remediation for each item.
applications.deliverable: A four-item hardening checklist with current state, remediation steps, and detection coverage for each.

M04.title: Practical applications — Symmetric Cryptography — AES, Block Modes, and Why AEAD Won

applications.title: Cipher Suite Selection Guide
applications.summary: Write a cipher suite selection decision guide for application developers using the AEAD-over-CBC principle.
applications.prompt: Draft a one-page cipher selection guide for developers integrating TLS or application-level encryption. The guide must: (1) explain why ECB is never acceptable with a diagram, (2) explain why CBC without MAC is unacceptable, (3) specify recommended AEAD options (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) with correct parameter requirements, (4) address nonce management and reuse.
applications.deliverable: A one-page developer guide with decision flowchart, code-level parameter recommendations, and a table of what-not-to-use with the reason for each.

M05.title: Practical applications — Key Exchange, Forward Secrecy, and TLS

applications.title: TLS Configuration Audit
applications.summary: Audit TLS configuration of three real services and classify each as preferred, acceptable, or a finding.
applications.prompt: Using testssl.sh, SSL Labs, or nmap ssl-enum-ciphers: scan three public-facing HTTPS services. For each service classify the TLS configuration as: (a) TLS 1.3 preferred, (b) TLS 1.2 with ECDHE+AEAD acceptable, or (c) TLS 1.2 with static RSA or CBC — a finding. For findings, state the exact RFC or OWASP reference that marks it deprecated.
applications.deliverable: A scan summary table with three services, their TLS version, cipher suites, forward secrecy status, and classification with justification.

M06.title: Practical applications — Hashing, Password Security, and PKI

applications.title: Password Storage Policy
applications.summary: Write a password storage policy document and implementation checklist for a web application.
applications.prompt: Draft a password storage policy that specifies: (1) the approved KDF (Argon2id with OWASP parameters), (2) why MD4/MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256 are prohibited for password storage, (3) the minimum acceptable bcrypt cost factor and why, (4) PKI certificate validation requirements for the application's TLS termination.
applications.deliverable: A policy document with implementation checklist, code snippet showing correct Argon2id usage, and a table of prohibited algorithms with the attack that breaks each.

M07.title: Practical applications — Linux Filesystem, Permissions, and Incident Response

applications.title: Linux Incident Investigation
applications.summary: Investigate a simulated Linux compromise using FHS navigation and bash log analysis pipelines.
applications.prompt: Given a Linux system (VM, Docker container, or log archive): (1) list all files in world-writable directories modified in the last 24 hours, (2) find all SUID binaries not in a known-good baseline, (3) write a bash one-liner to extract the top 10 source IPs with failed SSH login attempts from /var/log/auth.log, (4) check /etc/cron.d and ~/.config/systemd/ for unrecognised entries.
applications.deliverable: A four-section investigation report with findings, bash commands used, and a risk rating for each suspicious item found.

M08.title: Practical applications — Windows Authentication — NTLM, Kerberos, and Their Weak Links

applications.title: Windows Authentication Hardening Review
applications.summary: Review Windows authentication configuration for Pass-the-Hash and Kerberoasting exposure.
applications.prompt: On a Windows system or Active Directory lab: (1) verify NTLM is restricted (NTLMv2 minimum, LM disabled) via the relevant registry path, (2) enumerate service accounts with SPNs and check whether any use RC4 encryption, (3) verify Credential Guard or Restricted Admin mode is configured, (4) confirm Event 4769 auditing is enabled for Kerberos service ticket requests.
applications.deliverable: A hardening checklist with the registry path, current value, required value, and attack mitigated for each of the four items.

M09.title: Practical applications — Windows Telemetry, Persistence, and Detection

applications.title: Sysmon Detection Coverage Map
applications.summary: Build a detection coverage map linking Windows event IDs to the persistence mechanisms they detect.
applications.prompt: Create a detection coverage matrix: for each of the five persistence mechanisms from L26 (Run key, scheduled task, service installation, WMI subscription, COM hijacking), identify: (1) the Windows event IDs that detect the creation action, (2) the Sysmon event IDs that provide supplementary process context, (3) the minimum SIEM rule logic (field + value) to trigger an alert.
applications.deliverable: A five-row detection matrix with persistence mechanism, Windows event ID(s), Sysmon event ID(s), and SIEM rule logic for each.

M10.title: Practical applications — Layered Defense — Least Privilege, Control Bypasses, and the Detection Imperative

applications.title: Defence-in-Depth Control Stack
applications.summary: Design a layered defence stack for a Windows environment, pairing each preventative control with a detection capability.
applications.prompt: For a Windows Active Directory environment, design a defence-in-depth stack that addresses the course's attack taxonomy. For each layer specify: (1) the preventative control and its bypass condition, (2) the detection control that covers that bypass, (3) the least-privilege principle being enforced, (4) the MTTD target and how it is measured. The stack must include at minimum: MFA, privileged access, endpoint detection, and network segmentation.
applications.deliverable: A four-layer defence stack document with control, bypass, detection, least-privilege annotation, and MTTD target for each layer.

concepts.title: The Security Mindset
concepts.description: The habit of asking 'how could this be abused?' before 'how does this work?' — the inversion that defines security thinking.

concepts.title: Protocol Attack Surface
concepts.description: Every protocol feature embeds a trust assumption; that same assumption is the attack vector.

concepts.title: Trust Assumption Model
concepts.description: Identifying what a protocol trusts and who is allowed to supply that trusted value — the systematic method for deriving attack vectors.

concepts.title: TCP Attack Techniques
concepts.description: SYN flood exhausting the half-open connection table; RST injection terminating established connections.

concepts.title: ARP Spoofing
concepts.description: Exploiting ARP's trust-last-reply-wins model to intercept LAN traffic via gratuitous ARP replies.

concepts.title: HTTP Protocol Vulnerabilities
concepts.description: Host header injection and request smuggling exploiting CL/TE disagreement between proxy and backend.

concepts.title: DNS Architecture and Hierarchy
concepts.description: Recursive resolution chain: stub resolver → recursive resolver → root → TLD → authoritative; caching and TTL model.

concepts.title: DNS Attack Techniques
concepts.description: Cache poisoning (Kaminsky), DNS amplification, zone transfer abuse — all flowing from the caching trust model.

concepts.title: DNS C2 Detection
concepts.description: DNSSEC, CAA records, zone transfer restriction; Sysmon Event 22 for process-level DNS query attribution.

concepts.title: Symmetric Encryption
concepts.description: The one-time pad, AES as a block cipher, key sizes, and what a mode of operation does to a block cipher.

concepts.title: AES Cipher Modes
concepts.description: ECB, CBC, CTR, GCM — what each mode does and what differentiates their security properties.

concepts.title: CBC Mode Attacks
concepts.description: Padding oracle attacks, BEAST, POODLE, Lucky13 — all rooted in separating confidentiality from integrity.

concepts.title: AEAD Cipher Modes
concepts.description: AES-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305: combined encryption and authentication in a single operation, nonce management.

concepts.title: Key Exchange
concepts.description: The Diffie-Hellman problem: two parties agreeing on a shared secret over an untrusted channel.

concepts.title: Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange
concepts.description: Finite field DH vs ECDH, discrete logarithm hardness assumption, DH MITM vulnerability.

concepts.title: Forward Secrecy
concepts.description: Ephemeral ECDHE key pairs per session; session independence; harvest-now-decrypt-later attack mitigation.

concepts.title: TLS Version Policy
concepts.description: TLS 1.3 vs 1.2 — what changed, RFC 8446 obsoletes vs operational status, acceptable TLS 1.2 configurations.

concepts.title: Cryptographic Hash Functions
concepts.description: Preimage resistance, second-preimage resistance, collision resistance, avalanche effect; SHA-1 deprecation.

concepts.title: SHA-2 Family Standards
concepts.description: SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512 — correct uses for data integrity vs password storage vs HMAC.

concepts.title: Password Hashing KDFs
concepts.description: bcrypt, Argon2id — GPU resistance, memory hardness, OWASP parameters, salt vs no-salt implications.

concepts.title: PKI Certificate Chain Validation
concepts.description: RFC 5280 four-check validation: signature, validity, revocation, path length; root vs intermediate CA roles; CT logs.

concepts.title: Linux Filesystem Hierarchy
concepts.description: FHS as a forensic and security map: staging areas, persistence paths, log locations, /proc virtual filesystem.

concepts.title: Linux Permissions Model
concepts.description: rwx triplet, octal notation, SUID/SGID bits, sudo configuration — the security boundary and its bypass conditions.

concepts.title: SUID and Sudo Privilege Escalation
concepts.description: SUID binary enumeration, GTFOBins exploitation patterns, sudo misconfiguration paths to root.

concepts.title: Bash Log Analysis Pipelines
concepts.description: grep/awk/cut/sort/uniq pipelines for failed login extraction, IP ranking, sudo escalation detection.

concepts.title: NTLM and Kerberos Authentication
concepts.description: NTLM challenge-response with NT hash; Kerberos TGT/TGS exchange — both protocols from design to attack surface.

concepts.title: Windows Registry Security
concepts.description: HKLM vs HKCU roots, seven critical security paths: Run/RunOnce persistence, LSA, SAM, Kerberos encryption policy.

concepts.title: Pass-the-Hash Attack
concepts.description: NT hash as a direct authentication credential; LSASS extraction; lateral movement via shared local admin hashes.

concepts.title: Kerberoasting
concepts.description: Any domain user requests RC4-encrypted service tickets; offline crack via hashcat; gMSA as definitive defence.

concepts.title: Windows Security Event Logs
concepts.description: Security log event IDs 4624/4625/4688/4698/4769; System log 7045; PowerShell 4103/4104; SessionGUID correlation.

concepts.title: Windows Persistence Mechanisms
concepts.description: Run/RunOnce keys (HKCU no-admin), scheduled tasks (Event 4698), WMI subscriptions (Sysmon 19/20/21), COM hijacking.

concepts.title: Sysmon Endpoint Detection
concepts.description: Events 1/3/7/10/12/13/22 with ProcessGUID correlation; baseline-first exclusion strategy; macro malware chains.

concepts.title: Principle of Least Privilege
concepts.description: Minimum privilege, narrowest scope, shortest duration — applied across chmod, AD groups, service accounts, and LAPS.

concepts.title: Control Bypass and Safety vs Security
concepts.description: Every preventative control has a bypass condition; safety features (execution policy) vs security systems; LOLBins.

concepts.title: Layered Authentication Controls
concepts.description: FIDO2 origin-binding vs TOTP relay; Protected Users group enforcements; Conditional Access risk signals.

concepts.title: The Detection Imperative
concepts.description: Every preventative control must be paired with a detection capability; MTTD as the practitioner's primary metric.

paths.title: Complete Security Foundations
paths.description: All 31 lessons in module order. Full course coverage from security mindset through detection imperative.

paths.title: CTF and Offensive Techniques Fast Track
paths.description: Offensive attack patterns, exploitation techniques, and persistence mechanisms. Skips defensive-only lessons. ~15 lessons.

paths.title: Defensive Operations and Detection Focus
paths.description: Prioritises detection signals, telemetry correlation, and hardening controls. Full defensive context with just enough offensive coverage to understand what is being detected.

paths.title: Cryptography and PKI Track
paths.description: Complete cryptographic foundations: symmetric encryption, CBC attacks, AEAD, key exchange, forward secrecy, TLS policy, hash functions, password KDFs, and PKI chain validation.

paths.title: Windows Authentication and Endpoint Defence
paths.description: Windows-focused path: NTLM/Kerberos attack surface, registry forensics, Pass-the-Hash, Kerberoasting, event log correlation, persistence detection, and layered authentication controls.
